blocks.text: TUTORIAL ON POINTERS AND ARRAYS IN C by Ted Jensen Version 1.2 — February 2000 This material is in the public domain.
Translated from Spanish. Original available at: http://www.netcom.com/~tjensen/ptr/cpoint.htm
TABLE OF CONTENTS
Preface Introduction Chapter 1: What is a Pointer?

Chapter 2: Pointer Types and Arrays Chapter 3: Pointers and Strings Chapter 4: More on Strings Chapter 5: Pointers and Structures Chapter 6: More on Strings and Arrays of Strings Chapter 7: More on Multidimensional Arrays Chapter 8: Pointers to Arrays Chapter 9: Pointers and Dynamic Memory Management Chapter 10: Pointers to Functions Epilogue
PREFACE

This document aims to provide an introduction to pointers for beginning C programmers. After several years of reading and contributing to various C conferences, including those on FidoNet and UseNet, I have noticed that a good number of C beginners struggle to understand the fundamentals of pointers. This is why I have set out to try to explain them in simple language, with many examples.

The first version of this document was released to the public domain, as is this one. It was collected by Bob Stout, who included it as a file named PTR-HELP.TXT in his widely distributed SNIPPETS collection. Since that original 1995 edition, I have added a significant amount of material and corrected some minor errors.

In HTML version 1.1, I made some corrections in terminology handling as a result of feedback I received from around the world. In version 1.2, I updated the first 2 chapters to note the shift from 16-bit to 32-bit PC compilers.
Acknowledgments

So many people have unknowingly contributed to this work through the questions they posted in FidoNet C Echo, or in the UseNet newsgroup comp.lang.c, or in many other conferences on other networks, that it would be impossible to list them all. Special thanks to Bob Stout who was kind enough to include the first version of this file in his SNIPPETS.
About the Author

Ted Jensen is a retired Electronics Engineer who has worked both as a hardware designer and as a manager of hardware designers in the field of magnetic storage. Programming has been one of his hobbies since 1968, when he learned to punch cards to be run on a mainframe. (The mainframe had 64KB of magnetic memory!)
Use of This Material

Everything contained in this document is released to the public domain. Anyone is free to copy or distribute this material in whatever manner they prefer. The only thing I ask, in the event this material is used as support material in a class, is that it be distributed in its entirety — that is, including all chapters, the preface, and the introduction. I would also appreciate it if in that case the class instructor would send me a note at one of the addresses below informing me about it.

You do not have to be an instructor to contact me. I would greatly appreciate a message from anyone who finds this material useful, or who has constructive criticism to offer. I also hope to be able to answer questions sent by e-mail.
Ted Jensen Redwood City, CA 94064 tjensen@ix.netcom.com February 2000.
INTRODUCTION

If one wants to write efficient code in the C programming language, one must have a deep and active knowledge of pointer usage. Unfortunately, pointers in C seem to represent a stumbling block for beginners, particularly those coming from other programming languages such as Fortran, Pascal, or Basic.

It is to help these beginners with pointers that I have written the following material. To get the maximum benefit from it, I feel it is necessary for the user to be able to run the source code included in the articles. Because of this I have tried to keep all the code within ANSI specifications so that it can work on any ANSI-compatible compiler. I have tried to format the code within the text in such a way that, with the help of an ASCII text editor, one can copy the code block of interest to a new file and compile it on their system. I recommend readers do this, as it is very helpful for understanding the material.

Note: ANSI stands for American National Standards Institute. Through its X3J11 committee, formed in 1982, ANSI defined the C language standard in 1989, along with its library functions. This was done because several versions of the language had emerged that differed in features and extensions.
CHAPTER 1: WHAT IS A POINTER?

One of the most difficult things beginners in C find is understanding the concept of pointers. The purpose of this document is to provide an introduction to pointers and their use for these beginners.

I have often found that the main reason beginners have trouble with pointers is that they have a very poor or minimal understanding of variables — the way C makes use of them. So let us begin with a discussion of C variables in general.

A variable in a program is something with a name that contains a value which can vary. The way the compiler and linker handle this is by assigning a specific block of memory within the computer to store the value of a variable. The size of this block depends on the range that the variable is allowed to vary. For example, on 32-bit PCs, the size of an integer variable (int) is 4 bytes; on an older 16-bit machine, integers are 2 bytes in size. In C, the size of a variable type such as an integer does not have to be the same on all types of machines. In fact, in C we have different types of integer variables: there are long integers (long int) and short integers (short int), about which you can learn in any basic C text. This document assumes a 32-bit system with 4-byte integers.
If you want to know the sizes of the different integer types on your system, running the following code will give you the information:
When we declare a variable, we inform the compiler of two things: the name of the variable and the type. For example, we declare an integer variable named k by writing:
int k;

When the compiler encounters the word "int" in this statement, it reserves 4 bytes (on a PC) of memory to store the integer's value. It also builds a symbol table, and in that table adds the symbol k along with the corresponding memory address where those 4 bytes have been reserved.
So if we later write:
we expect to find, at execution time, a 2 placed in the memory area reserved for holding the value of k. In C we refer to a variable like the integer k as an "object".

It makes sense to say that there are two values associated with the object k: one is the value of the integer stored there (a 2 in the example above), and the other is the "value" of the memory location where it has been stored — i.e., the address of k. Some texts refer to these two values using the nomenclature rvalue (right value) and lvalue (left value).

In some languages, the lvalue is the value permitted on the left side of the assignment operator '=' (the address where the result of the evaluation of the right-side expression will be stored). The rvalue is the one found on the right side of the assignment — the 2 above. Rvalues cannot be used on the left side of an assignment statement. Thus doing something like: 2 = k; is not permitted.
Actually, the above definition of "lvalue" is modified somewhat for C. According to K&R II (page 197):

"An object is a region of storage; an lvalue is an expression referring to an object." For now, the definition above suffices. As we become more familiar with pointers, we will go into more detail on this.
Now let us consider:

int j, k;
k = 2;

j = 7;    /* line 1 */ k = j;    /* line 2 */ In the above, the compiler interprets j in line 1 as the address of the variable j (its lvalue) and creates code to copy the value 7 to that address. In line 2, however, j is interpreted as its rvalue (since it is on the right side of the assignment operator '='). This means j here refers to the value stored at the memory address assigned to j, 7 in this case. So 7 is copied to the address designated by the lvalue of k.

In these examples we have been using 4-byte integers to store numbers, so copying rvalues from one storage address to another is done by copying 4 bytes. If we were using 2-byte integers, we would be copying 2 bytes each time.

Now, suppose for some reason we want a variable to store an lvalue (an address). The size required to store such a value depends on the system. On older computers with only 64KB of total memory, the address of any location in memory can fit in 2 bytes. Computers with more memory may need more bytes to store an address. The actual size required is not so important as long as we can tell the compiler that what we want to store is an address.

This kind of variable is known as a "pointer variable" (for reasons that will hopefully become clear a little later). In C, when we define a pointer variable, we do so by preceding its name with an asterisk. In C we also give our pointer a type, which in this case refers to the type of data stored at the address we will hold in our pointer. For example, consider the following variable declaration:

int *ptr; ptr is the name of our variable (just as k was the name of our integer variable). The * informs the compiler that what we want is a pointer variable — that is, that the necessary bytes should be reserved to hold a memory address. The "int" means we want to use our pointer variable to store the address of an integer. It is said that such a pointer type "points to" an integer. Note, however, that when we wrote int k; we did not assign a value to k.

If the declaration is made outside any function, ANSI compilers will automatically initialize it to zero. Similarly, ptr has no assigned value — that is, we have not stored an address in the declaration above. Again, if the declaration were made outside any function, it is initialized to a value guaranteed not to point to any C object or function. A pointer initialized this way is defined as a "null pointer".

The actual bit pattern for a null pointer may or may not evaluate to zero, as it depends specifically on the system on which the code is being developed. To make source code portable across different compilers on various systems, a macro is used to represent a null pointer. This macro is named NULL. So setting the value of a pointer using this macro, with a statement like ptr = NULL, guarantees that the pointer is a null pointer. Similarly, just as we check for zero in an integer variable with if (k == 0), we can check that a pointer is null using if (ptr == NULL).
But, returning to the use of our new variable ptr. Suppose now we want to store in ptr the address of our integer variable k. To do so we use the unary operator & and write:
ptr = &k;

What the & operator does is retrieve the address of k, even though k is on the right side of the assignment operator '=', and copies that address into the content of our pointer ptr. Now, ptr "points to" k. There is one more operator to discuss: the "indirection" (or dereference) operator is the asterisk and is used as follows:
*ptr = 7;

This will copy 7 to the address pointed to by ptr. So since ptr "points to" (contains the address of) k, the statement above will assign k the value 7. That is, when we use the '*' we refer to the value ptr is pointing to, not the value of the pointer itself.
Similarly we could write:
printf("%d\n", *ptr);
to print to the screen the integer value stored at the address to which ptr points.
A way to see how all this fits together would be to run the following program, carefully reviewing the code and output.
PROGRAM 1.1
/* Program 1.1 from PTRTUT10.TXT 6/10/97 */

#include <stdio.h>
int j, k;
int *ptr;
return 0;
Note: We still need to discuss the aspects of C that require the use of the (void *) expression used here. For now, include it in the test code. We will explain the reasons for this expression later.
Summary
A variable is declared by giving it a type and a name (e.g., int k;).

A pointer variable is declared by giving it a type and a name (e.g., int *ptr), where the asterisk tells the compiler that the variable named ptr is a pointer variable, and the type tells the compiler what type of variable our pointer will point to (int in this case).
Once a variable has been declared, we can get its address by prepending the unary operator & to its name, as in &k.
We can "dereference" a pointer — that is, refer to the value stored at the address it points to — using the unary operator '*', as in *ptr.
The "lvalue" of a variable is the value of its address — i.e., the memory location where the value it contains is stored. The "rvalue" of a variable is the value stored at that address.
References for Chapter 1:
[1] B. Kernighan and D. Ritchie, "The C Programming Language", 2nd Edition, Prentice Hall. ISBN 0-13-110362-8
CHAPTER 2: POINTER TYPES AND ARRAYS
Let us consider why we need to identify the "type" of variable a pointer points to, as in:
int *ptr; One reason for doing this is that once ptr points to something and we write:
*ptr = 2;

the compiler will know how many bytes to copy to the memory location pointed to by ptr. If ptr were declared as a pointer to an integer, 4 bytes would be copied. Similarly for floats and doubles, the appropriate number of bytes would be copied. But defining the type the pointer points to enables a number of interesting ways for the compiler to interpret the code. For example, consider a block of memory consisting of 10 integers in a row — that is, 40 bytes of memory are reserved to hold 10 integers.
Say we now point our integer pointer ptr to the first of these integers. Moreover, suppose this first integer is stored at memory address 100 (decimal). Then what happens when we write:

ptr + 1; Since the compiler "knows" that this is a pointer (its value is a memory address) and that it points to an integer (its current address, 100, is the address where an integer is stored), it adds 4 to ptr instead of 1, so ptr points to the next integer, at memory position 104. Similarly, if ptr were declared as a pointer to a short integer, it would add 2 instead of 1. The same goes for other data types such as floats, doubles, or even user-defined types such as structures. This is not obviously the "normal" type of addition we are used to. In C, it is known as "pointer arithmetic", something we will see a bit later.

Likewise, since ++ptr and ptr++ are equivalent to ptr + 1 (although the moment in the program when ptr is incremented differs), incrementing a pointer using the unary increment operator ++, whether pre- or post-, increments the address by the amount sizeof(type) where "type" is the type of the object pointed to (for example 4 for an integer).
And since a block of 10 contiguously arranged integers in memory is, by definition, an array of integers, this reveals an interesting relationship between arrays and pointers.
Consider the following:

We then have an array containing six integers. We refer to each of these integers by means of a subscript to my_array, i.e., using my_array[0] through my_array[5]. But we can access them in an alternative way using a pointer like this:

int *ptr; ptr = &my_array[0]; /* point our pointer to the first integer in our array */ And then we can print the values of our array using either array notation or by "dereferencing" our pointer. The following code illustrates this concept.
PROGRAM 2.1

#include <stdio.h>
int my_array[] = {1, 23, 17, 4, -5, 100};
int *ptr;
return 0;

Compile and run the above program and note how lines marked A and B print the same values. Note also how we dereference our pointer ptr in line B: we first add i to it and then dereference the new pointer. Change line B so it reads:
printf("ptr + %d = %d\n", i, *ptr++);
Compile and run again, then modify it again to:
printf("ptr + %d = %d\n", i, *(++ptr));
Compile and run once more. Try to predict what will appear on screen each time and carefully review the actual output.
In C, the standard states that where we use &array_name[0] we can replace it with array_name. So in the example code, what we wrote as:
ptr = &my_array[0];
can be written as:
ptr = my_array;

and we get the same result. This leads many texts to say that the name of an array is a pointer. While this is true, I prefer to think that "the name of an array is the address of the first element that the array contains." Many beginners (including myself when I was learning) tend to confuse themselves by thinking of it as a pointer.
For example, although we can do ptr = my_array; we cannot do:
my_array = ptr;
The reason is that while ptr is a variable, my_array is a constant. That is, the address at which the first element of my_array will be stored cannot be changed once my_array[] has been declared.

Earlier, when discussing the term "lvalue", it was stated that: "An object is a region of storage; an lvalue is an expression referring to an object." This raises an interesting problem. Since my_array is a named region of storage, why isn't my_array in the assignment above an lvalue? To resolve this problem, some refer to things like my_array as a "non-modifiable lvalue".
Let us modify the example program by changing: ptr = &my_array[0]; to ptr = my_array; and run again to verify that the results are identical.

Let us dig a bit deeper into the difference between the names ptr and my_array as we did above. Some writers refer to the name of an array as a constant pointer. What do we mean by this? Well, to understand the term "constant" in this context, let us return to our definition of the term "variable". When we declare a variable, we reserve a place in memory to store the value of the appropriate type. Once this is done, the name of the variable can be interpreted in one of two ways. When used on the left side of the assignment operator, the compiler interprets it as the memory address at which to place the result of the evaluation of what is on the right side. But when used on the right side, the name of a variable is interpreted to represent the contents of the memory address reserved to hold the value of that variable.
With this in mind, let us analyze the simplest of constants, as in:

int i, k;
i = 2;

Here, while i is a variable and occupies space in the data section of memory, 2 is a constant and as such, rather than occupying memory in the data segment, it is embedded directly in the code section of memory. This means that when we write something like k = i; we tell the compiler to create code that at runtime looks at the address &i to determine the value to be moved to k, while code created by something like i = 2 simply puts the 2 in the code (no reference is made to the data segment). This is because both k and i are objects, but 2 is not.

Similarly, since my_array is a constant, once the compiler determines where the array will be stored in memory, it already "knows" the address of my_array[0], and when it encounters: ptr = my_array; it simply uses this address as a constant in the code segment — nothing more (no reference is made to the data segment).

This would be a good place to explain the use of the (void *) expression in Program 1.1. As we have seen, we can have pointers of different types. In the following lessons we will see pointers to structures and even pointers to pointers.

We have already learned that the size of a pointer can vary between systems. It is also possible that the size of a pointer may vary depending on the type of data object it points to. So, just as with integers where we can have problems when assigning, for example, a long integer value to a short integer variable, we can similarly encounter problems when trying to assign values from one type of pointer to a pointer of another type.
To reduce this problem, C offers a void type pointer. We can declare a pointer of this type by writing something like:

void *vptr; A void pointer is a kind of generic pointer. For example, while C does not allow comparison between a pointer to an integer and a pointer to a character, each of these can be compared with a void pointer.

Of course, as with other variable types, casts can be used to convert one type of pointer to another under appropriate circumstances. In Program 1.1, I converted integer pointers to void pointers to make them compatible with the %p conversion specifier. In later chapters, other conversions will be made for the reasons explained here.

Well, there has been quite a lot of technical material to digest, and I do not expect a beginner to understand all of this on a first reading. With time and experimentation you will surely come back to re-read the first two chapters. But for now, let us continue and discuss the existing relationship between pointers, character arrays, and strings.
CHAPTER 3: POINTERS AND STRINGS

The study of strings is useful for deepening the relationship between pointers and arrays. It also facilitates demonstrating how some of C's standard string functions can be implemented. Finally, it will illustrate how and when pointers can and should be passed to a function.

In C, strings are arrays of characters. This is not necessarily true in other languages. In Basic, Pascal, Fortran, and others, a string has its own data type. But in C this is not the case. In C, a string is an array of characters terminated with a binary zero character (written as \0). To begin our discussion, let us write some code which, while preferred for purely illustrative purposes, you probably would not write in a real program. Consider, for example:

Although one would never build strings this way, the end result is a string that is actually an array of characters terminated with a null character. By definition in C, a string is an array of characters terminated with the null character. Note that nul is not the same as NULL. The "nul" refers to a zero defined by the escape sequence '\0'. That is, it occupies one byte of memory. "NULL", on the other hand, is the name of the macro used to initialize null pointers. NULL is defined in a C compiler header file, while nul may not be defined at all.
Since writing code like the above would take a lot of time, C allows two alternative ways to get the same result. The first would be to write:
char my_string[40] = {'T', 'e', 'd', '\0'};
But this involves more typing than is convenient. So C permits:
char my_string[40] = "Ted";
When we use double quotes instead of single quotes as in the previous examples, the null character ('\0') is automatically added to the end of the string.

In any of the cases described above the same thing occurs: the compiler assigns a continuous memory block of 40 bytes in length to hold the characters, and initializes it so that the first 4 characters are T, e, d, \0.
Let us now look at the following program:
PROGRAM 3.1
/* Program 3.1 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */

char *pA;  /* a pointer to type character */ char *pB;  /* another pointer to type character */ puts(strA);        /* display the string strA */ pA = strA;         /* point pA at string strA */ puts(pA);          /* display where pA points */ pB = strB;         /* point pB at string strB */ putchar('\n');    /* blank line */ while (*pA != '\0')  /* line A */
*pB++ = *pA++;   /* line B */
*pB = '\0';          /* line C */ puts(strB);          /* display strB on screen */ return 0;

What we did above was to start by defining two 80-character arrays. Since these are defined globally, they are initialized to \0 first. Then strA has its first characters initialized to the string between quotation marks.

Now, in the code, we declare two character pointers and display the string on screen. We then point pA to strA. This means that, by the meaning of the assignment operation, we copy the memory address of strA[0] into our pointer variable pA. We then use puts() to display what we are pointing to with pA on screen. Consider that the prototype of the puts() function is:
int puts(const char *s);

For the moment, let us ignore the "const". The parameter passed to puts() is a pointer — that is, the value of a pointer (since in C all parameters are passed by value), and since the value of a pointer is the memory address it points to, or to put it simply: an address. So when we write puts(strA); as we have seen, we are passing the address of strA[0].
Similarly when we do puts(pA); we are passing the same address, since we had established pA = strA.
Let us continue examining the code up to the while() on line A:

Line A says: "While the character pointed to by pA (i.e., *pA) is not a null character (which is '\0'), do the following." Line B says: "Copy the character pointed to by pA (i.e., *pA) to the space pointed to by pB, then increment pA so that it points to the next character, and likewise increment pB so that it points to the next space." Once we have copied the last character, pA now points to the null terminator and the loop ends. However, we have not copied the string termination character. And by definition, a string in C must end with a null character. So we add nul with line C.

It is truly instructive to run this program in a debugger while watching strA, strB, pA, and pB, stepping through each step of the program. It is also good to try initializing strB[] to a string instead of just declaring it — something like strB[80] = "12345678901234567890123456789012345678901234567890", where the number of digits is greater than the length of strA, and then repeat the step-by-step observation while watching the variable contents. Try it!

Returning to the prototype for puts() for a moment: the "const" used as a parameter informs the user that the function will not modify the string pointed to by s — i.e., it will treat that string as a constant.

Of course, what the above program does is a simple way to copy a string. After playing with this a bit and once you have a good understanding of what is happening, we will then proceed to create our replacement for the standard strcpy() function that comes with C. It would look like this:
char *my_strcpy(char *destination, char *source)

{
char *p = destination;
while (*source != '\0')
return destination;

In this case I have followed the procedure used in the standard routine of returning a pointer to the destination. Again, the function is designed to accept values of two character pointers — that is, addresses — and because of this, in the previous program we could have written:
I have deviated slightly from the form used in the standard C function, which has the prototype:
char *my_strcpy(char *destination, const char *source);

Here the "const" modifier is used to ensure that the function will not modify the contents pointed to by the source pointer. You can test this by modifying the function above and its prototype to include the "const" modifier as shown. Then inside the function you can add code that tries to change the contents of what is pointed to by source — something like: *source = 'X'; which would normally change the first character of the string to 'X'. The const modifier causes the compiler to detect this as an error. Try it and see.

Let us now consider some things the above example has demonstrated. First, consider the fact that *ptr++ is interpreted as returning the value pointed to by ptr and then incrementing the value of the pointer. This has to do with operator precedence. If we had written (*ptr)++ we would not be incrementing the pointer, but rather what it contains! That is, if we used it on the first character of the string in the example, the 'T' would be incremented to a 'U'. You can write simple code to demonstrate this.

Remember again that a string is nothing more than an array of characters, with its last character being '\0'. What we have done is ideal for copying an array. It happens that we have done it with a character array, but the same technique can be applied to an array of integers, doubles, etc. In those cases, we would not be dealing with strings, and then the array would not need to be marked with a special value like the null character. We could implement a version based on a special terminator to identify the end. For example, we could copy an array of positive integers and mark the end with a negative integer. On the other hand, it is more usual that when we write a function to copy an array that is not a string, we indicate the number of elements to be copied along with the address of the array. For example, something like what the following prototype would indicate:
void int_copy(int *ptrA, int *ptrB, int nbr);

where nbr is the number of integer elements to be copied. You can play around with this idea, create an array of integers, and try to implement an integer copy function int_copy() and make it work correctly.

This allows the use of functions to manipulate large arrays. For example, if we had an array of 5000 integers that we wanted to manipulate with a function, we would only need to pass that function the address where the array is stored (and some auxiliary information like nbr above, depending on what we are going to do). The array itself is not passed to the function — that is, it is not copied and put on the stack before calling the function. Only the address where its first element is located (the array address is passed to the function).

This is different from passing, say, an integer to a function. When we pass an integer variable to a function, what happens is that we make a copy of this integer — we get its value and put it on the stack. Inside the function, any manipulation done on this variable will not affect the contents of the original variable. But with arrays and pointers we can pass the addresses of variables to a function, and thus manipulate the values contained in the original variables.
CHAPTER 4: MORE ON STRINGS
Well, we have progressed quite a bit in a short time. Let us step back and look at what we did in Chapter 3 when copying strings, but from a different perspective. Consider the following function:
char *my_strcpy(char destination[], char source[])

{
int i = 0;
while (source[i] != '\0')

{
destination[i] = source[i];

}
destination[i] = '\0';
return destination;

Recall that strings are arrays of characters. Here we have chosen to use array notation instead of pointer notation to do the copy. The result is the same — that is, the string is copied in the same way using this notation as it did before. This exposes some interesting points to discuss.

Since parameters are passed by value, whether passing the character pointer pointing to the address of the array or the array name as above, what is actually being passed is the address of the first element of the array in each case. This means that the numerical value of the parameter passed is the same whether we pass a character pointer or the array name as a parameter. This would imply in some way that: source[i] is the same as *(p + i).

This is true — wherever one writes a[i] one can replace it with *(a + i) without any problem. In fact the compiler will create the same code in either case. From this we realize that pointer arithmetic is the same as using array subscripts. Either syntax produces the same result.

This does NOT mean that pointers and arrays are the same, because they are not. We are only pointing out that to identify a given element of an array we have the option to use two different syntaxes — one using array subscripts and the other using pointer arithmetic — which leads to the same result.

Now, analyzing the last expression, the part that says (a + i), is a simple addition using the + operator, and the rules of C say that such an expression is commutative. So (a + i) is the same as (i + a), so we can write *(i + a) just as well as *(a + i).
But *(i + a) could have come from i[a]! From all this we obtain a curious truth such that if:

Try it! Initialize an array of characters, integers, longs, etc. and using the conventional method assign a value to the 3rd or 4th element and print that value to confirm it is stored. Then invert the notation as done above. A good compiler will not complain with an error or warning message, and the same results will be obtained. A curiosity... and nothing more!
Now going back to our function above, we wrote:
destination[i] = source[i];
Due to the fact that using array subscripts or pointer arithmetic leads to the same result, we could have written this as:
*(destination + i) = *(source + i);

But this implies 2 additions for each value i takes. Additions generally take more time than increments (as done using ++ in i++). This is not necessarily true with modern optimizing compilers, but one cannot always be sure. So it is possible that the pointer version is slightly faster than the array version.

Another way to speed up the pointer version would be to change: while (*source != '\0') to simply while (*source), since the value within the parentheses will become zero (FALSE) at the same time in either case.

At this point you may want to experiment a bit by writing your own programs using pointers. Manipulating text strings is a good way to experiment. Perhaps you could implement your own version of the standard library functions from <string.h> such as strlen(), strcat(), strchr(), and any others you may have on your system.
We will come back to strings and their manipulation through pointers in a future chapter. For now, let us continue and discuss structures.
CHAPTER 5: POINTERS AND STRUCTURES

As you may know, it is possible to declare the form of a data block containing different data types by means of a structure declaration. For example, a personnel file would contain structures that would look something like:

Suppose we have many of these structures in a disk file and we want to read each one and print the first and last name of each, so that we have a list with the name of each person in the file. The remaining information is not to be printed. We want to do this by means of a function to which we pass a pointer to the structure as parameter. For didactic purposes I will only use one structure for now. But let us focus on the fact that the objective is to implement the function, not to read from a disk file, which we presumably already know how to do.
Recall that we can access members of a structure by means of the '.' (dot) operator.
PROGRAM 5.1
/* Program 5.1 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
return 0;

Now, this particular structure is very small compared to those used in many C programs. To the above we might want to add (without showing the particular data types): Address, Phone, Zip Code, City, Marital Status, Social Security Number, etc.

If we have a considerable number of employees, it would be ideal to handle the data within these structures through functions. For example, we want to implement a function that prints the name of employees, contained in whatever structure is passed to it. However, in the original C language (Kernighan & Ritchie, 1st Edition) it was not possible to pass a structure as a parameter to a function — only a pointer pointing to a structure. In ANSI C it is now possible to pass a complete structure. But since our goal is to learn about pointers, we will not pursue that now.

In any case, if we pass the complete structure it means we must copy the contents of the structure from the calling function to the called function. In systems that use stacks, this is done by pushing the contents of the structure onto the stack. With large structures this can be a problem. However, passing pointers uses a minimum of stack space.
Since we are discussing pointers, let us discuss how to pass a pointer to a structure to a function and how to use it within the function.

Consider the case described: we want a function that accepts as a parameter a pointer to a structure, and inside that function we want to access the members of the structure. For example, we want to print the name of the employee from our example structure.
Since we know our pointer is going to point to a structure declared using struct record, we declare said pointer with:
struct record *st_ptr;
And make it point to our example structure with:
st_ptr = &my_record;

Now we can access a member of the structure by dereferencing the pointer. But how do we dereference a pointer to a structure? Well, consider that we want to use the pointer to change the age of the employee. For this we would write:
(*st_ptr).age = 63;
Note carefully. It says: replace what is in parentheses with what st_ptr is pointing to, which is the structure my_record. So this reduces to the same as my_record.age.
However, this notation is not very commonly used, and the creators of C have provided the possibility of using an alternative syntax with the same meaning:
st_ptr -> age = 63;
With this in mind, look at the following program.
PROGRAM 5.2
/* Program 5.2 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
return 0;

Again, this is a lot of information to absorb at once. I suggest the reader compile and run the programs and if possible use a debugger to view the state of variables by stepping through the program step by step through the main function and following the code into the show_name() function to see what is happening.
CHAPTER 6: MORE ON STRINGS AND ARRAYS OF STRINGS
Well, let us return to strings. In what follows all declarations are understood as made globally — that is, they are made outside any function, including main().
We said in a previous chapter that we could do:
char my_name[40] = "Ted";
With which we would reserve space to hold an array of 40 bytes and place the string in the first 4 bytes of the array (3 for the characters in quotes and one more for '\0').
If we really only wanted an array to hold the name "Ted", we can do:
char my_name[] = "Ted";

And the compiler would count the characters, leaving room for the null terminator, and would then store a total of 4 characters in memory, the address of which would be returned by the array name, in this case my_name.
Sometimes instead of code like the above we will find:
char *my_name = "Ted";

Which is an alternate declaration. Is there a difference between them? The answer is... yes. Using array notation, 4 bytes of storage in the static memory block are taken — one for each character and one for the null terminator.
But in pointer notation the same 4 bytes are required plus N bytes to hold the pointer variable my_name (where N depends on the system but is usually a minimum of 2 bytes and may be 4 or more).

In array notation, "my_name" is shorthand for &my_name[0], which is the address of the first element of the array. Since the address at which the array will be stored will be fixed at program execution time, this is a constant (not a variable).
In pointer notation, my_name is a variable. Deciding which method to use and which is better depends on what you plan to do in the rest of the program.
Let us go one step further and consider what would happen if each of these declarations were made inside a function, as opposed to globally outside the scope of any function:

In the case of my_function_A, the content, or value(s), of array a[] are considered as data. The array has been initialized to the values ABCDE. In the case of my_function_B, the value of pointer cp is considered as data. The pointer has been initialized to point to the string FGHIJ. In both functions the definitions are local variables, and therefore the string ABCDE is stored on the stack, as is the value of pointer cp. The string FGHIJ is stored wherever is appropriate — on my system it is stored in the data segment.

Incidentally, the initialization of arrays of automatic variables as done in my_function_A was illegal in the old K&R C, and only "came to be" in the new ANSI C. A factor that may be important when considering portability and backward compatibility.
As we discuss the relationships/differences between pointers and arrays, let us look at something about multidimensional arrays. Consider for example the array:
char multi[5][10];

What about that? Well, let us look at it from another perspective. Take as the "name" of the array the underlined portion. Then, prepending char and appending [10], we have an array of 10 characters. But the name multi[5] is itself an array indicating it consists of 5 elements which in turn consist of 10 characters each. Therefore we have an array of 5 arrays of 10 characters each.

Assuming we have filled this two-dimensional array, in memory we would have something similar to having the two-dimensional array formed by 5 separate arrays that we had initialized with something like:
multi[0][3] = '3' multi[1][7] = 'h' multi[4][0] = 'J' Since arrays are contiguous in memory, our block of memory must look something like this in reality:
0123456789abcdefghijABCDEFGHIJ9876543210JIHGFEDCBA Starting at address &multi[0][0].

Note that I did not write multi[0] = "0123456789". Had I done so, a string terminator '\0' would have been added at the end, since wherever there are double quotes a null character is added at the end of the characters within those quotes.

If this were the case I would have had to declare the array to have space for 11 characters per row instead of 10. This is because this is a two-dimensional array of characters, NOT an array of strings.

Now, the compiler knows how many columns the array has so it can interpret multi + 1 as the address of 'a' in the second row of the above array. That is, it adds 10 — the number of columns — to get this address. If we were working with integers and the array were the same dimensions, the compiler would add 10 * sizeof(int), which on my machine is 20 (using 2-byte integers). So, the address of '9' in the fourth row would be &multi[3][0] or *(multi + 3) in pointer notation. To get the content of the 2nd element in the 4th row we add 1 to this address and dereference it: *(*(multi + 3) + 1).
Thinking about it a bit, we can observe that:

*(*(multi + row) + column)  -- pointer access to array element multi[row][column]           -- array notation -- both are equivalent The following program demonstrates the use of a matrix of integers instead of characters.
PROGRAM 6.1
/* Program 6.1 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
#include <stdio.h>
#define ROWS 5 #define COLS 10
for (row = 0; row < ROWS; row++) for (col = 0; col < COLS; col++)
multi[row][col] = row * col;
for (row = 0; row < ROWS; row++) for (col = 0; col < COLS; col++)
return 0;

Due to the double dereferencing required in the pointer version, the name of a two-dimensional array is said to be equivalent to a pointer to a pointer. With 3-dimensional arrays we would be talking about arrays of arrays of arrays, and the name of such would be the equivalent of a pointer to a pointer to a pointer.
However, here we have initially reserved the memory block for the array using array notation. So we are dealing with a constant, not a variable — this means we are talking about a fixed address.
The dereferencing used above allows us to access any element in the array of arrays without needing to change the address value (the address of multi[0][0] is provided by the symbol multi).
CHAPTER 7: MORE ON MULTIDIMENSIONAL ARRAYS
In the previous chapter we noted that once given:
#define ROWS 5 #define COLS 10
int multi[ROWS][COLS];
we can access individual elements of the array multi using either:
multi[row][col] *(*(multi + row) + col) To better understand what is happening, let us replace *(multi + row) with X, such that the expression becomes *(X + col).

We now see that X is like a pointer since the expression is being dereferenced and we know that col is an integer. The arithmetic to be used here is of a special type called "pointer arithmetic". This means that, since we are dealing with an array of integers, the address to be pointed to by (the value of) X + col + 1 must be greater than the address of X + col by an amount equal to sizeof(int).

Since we know the memory structure for two-dimensional arrays, we can determine that in the expression multi + row, multi + row + 1 causes this to increment by a value equal to that needed to "point to" the next row, which would then be COLS * sizeof(int).

This tells us that if the expression *(*(multi + row) + col) is to be evaluated correctly at runtime, the compiler must generate code that takes the value of COLS — i.e., the second dimension — into account. Due to the equivalence between the two forms of expression, this is true whether we use pointer syntax or array syntax multi[row][col].
So to evaluate either of these two expressions, 5 values must be known:
The address of the first element of the array, which is known through the expression multi — i.e., the name of the array.
The size and type of the elements that make up the array — in this case sizeof(int).
The second dimension of the array.
The specific value of the index for the first dimension, row in this case.
The specific value of the index for the second dimension, col in this case.

Once we know this, let us consider the problem of designing a function that manipulates the elements of a previously declared array. For example, one that would set all elements of the array multi to 1:
for (row = 0; row < ROWS; row++) for (col = 0; col < COLS; col++)

m_array[row][col] = 1;
}
And to call this function we would use:
set_value(multi);

Inside this function, we have used the values established by #define for ROWS and COLS, which set the loop limits. But as far as the compiler is concerned, these are simply constants — there is nothing directly connecting them to the size of the array within the function. row and col are local variables, of course. The formal definition of the parameter allows the compiler to determine the characteristics of the pointer value that will be passed at runtime.

We do not really need the first dimension, and as we will see later, there will be occasions where we will prefer not to declare it in the function parameter definitions — not that it should become a habit or something to be consistent with. But the second dimension must be used as shown in the parameter expression. The reason we need it is because of the way m_array[row][col] is evaluated.

While the parameter defines the data type (int in this case) and the automatic variables for row and col are defined in the for loops, only one value can be passed using a single parameter. In this case, it is the value of multi as passed in the function call — that is, the address of its first element, more properly referred to as a pointer to the array. So the only way we have of informing the compiler of the second dimension is to include it explicitly in the function's parameter definition.

In fact, generally all dimensions of order greater than one need multidimensional arrays. This means that if we are talking about 3-dimensional arrays, the second and third dimensions must be specified in the parameter definition.
CHAPTER 8: POINTERS TO ARRAYS

Of course pointers can point to any data type, including arrays. While this was already evident when discussing Program 3.1, it is important to expand on how we do this when dealing with multidimensional arrays.
To review, in Chapter 2, we established that, given an array of integers, we can point an integer pointer to that array using:

int *ptr; ptr = &my_array[0]; /* point our pointer to the first element of the array */ As we established, the type of the pointer variable must match the type of the first element in the array. In addition, we can use a pointer as a parameter of a function designed to manipulate an array. For example, given:

int array[3] = {1, 5, 7};
void a_func(int *p);

Some programmers prefer to write the prototype of a function as: void a_func(int p[]); which would inform other users of this function that the function serves to manipulate array elements. Of course, in either case what we are actually passing to the function is a pointer to the first element of the array, regardless of the notation used in the function prototype or definition. Note that if we use array notation, there is no need to pass the actual dimension of the array, since we are not passing the complete array, only the address of its first element.

Let us move on to the problem of dealing with a two-dimensional array. As stated in the last chapter, C interprets a 2-dimensional array as if it were an array consisting of a one-dimensional array. In that case, the first element of a 2-dimensional array of integers is a one-dimensional array of integers. And a pointer to a two-dimensional array of integers must point to that type of data. One way to accomplish this is through the use of the typedef keyword. typedef assigns a new name to the specified data type. For example:
typedef unsigned char byte;
makes the name byte mean the data type unsigned char. Therefore:
byte b[10]; would then be an array of 10 elements of type unsigned char.

Notice how in the typedef declaration, the word byte has replaced what would normally be the name of our unsigned char variable. Therefore, the rule for using typedef is that the name of the new data type is the name used in the type definition.
So when declaring:
typedef int Array[10];

Array becomes a new data type for an array of 10 integers. That is, Array my_array declares my_array as an array of 10 integers, and Array arr2d[5]; makes arr2d an array of 5 arrays of 10 integers each.

Note that making Array *p1d; makes p1d a pointer to an array of 10 elements. Since *p1d points to the same type of data as arr2d, assigning the address of the two-dimensional array arr2d to p1d, the pointer to a one-dimensional array of 10 integers, is acceptable. That is, both p1d = &arr2d[0]; and p1d = arr2d; are correct.

Since the data type we use for our pointer is an array of 10 integers, we would expect incrementing p1d by 1 to change its value by 10 * sizeof(int), and it does. That means sizeof(*p1d) is 40. You can verify this yourself by writing a small program.

Using typedef makes things clearer for the reader and easier for the programmer, but it is not strictly necessary. What is needed is a way to declare a pointer like p1d without using the typedef keyword. It is possible to do this, and:
is the appropriate declaration — i.e., p1d here is a pointer to an array of 10 integers just as it was when declared using the Array data type. Note that it differs from:
int *p1d[10]; which would make p1d the name of an array of 10 pointers to integer type.
CHAPTER 9: POINTERS AND DYNAMIC MEMORY MANAGEMENT
There are times when it is convenient to reserve memory at runtime using malloc(), calloc(), or any other memory allocation function.

Using this method allows us to postpone the decision about the size of the memory block needed to store, for example, an array, until runtime. Or it allows us to use a section of memory to store an array of integers at a given time, and later, when that memory is no longer needed, free it for other uses — such as to store an array of structures.

When memory is allocated, the allocation functions (such as malloc() or calloc(), etc.) return a pointer. The type of this pointer depends on whether we are using an old K&R compiler or one of the new compilers with ANSI specifications. With the old compiler type, the pointer returned is of type char, while with ANSI ones it is of type void.

If you are using one of these old compilers and want to reserve memory for an integer array, you have to cast the char type pointer to an integer (int) type pointer. For example, to reserve space for 10 integers, we would write:
int *iptr;
iptr = (int *)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));

if (iptr == NULL) { ... Error handling routine goes here ... } If we are using an ANSI-compatible compiler, malloc() returns a void type pointer, and since a pointer of this type can be assigned to point to a variable of any object type, the (int *) cast shown in the code above is not necessary. The size of the array can be determined at runtime so it is not necessary to know this data at compile time. This means that the 10 above can be a variable read from a data file, from the keyboard, or calculated based on a need, at runtime.
Due to the equivalence between array notation and pointer notation, once pointer iptr has been assigned as above, we can use array notation. For example, one can write:
int k;
ptr[k] = 2;
to set the value of all elements to 2.

Even with a good understanding of pointers and arrays, something that commonly trips up C beginners is the dynamic allocation of memory for multidimensional arrays. In general, we would like to be able to access elements of such arrays using array notation, not pointer notation, whenever possible. Depending on the application we may or may not know the dimensions of an array at compile time. This leads to a variety of approaches to solving our task.

As we have seen, when dynamically allocating a one-dimensional array, its dimension can be determined at runtime. Now for the dynamic allocation of higher-order arrays, we will never need to know the first dimension at compile time. Whether we need to know the other dimensions depends on how we write the code. Let us discuss various methods of dynamically allocating memory to two-dimensional integer arrays. To begin, consider the case where the second dimension is known at compile time:
METHOD 1
One way to tackle this problem is by using the typedef keyword. To allocate 2-dimensional arrays, recall that the following two notations produce the same object code:

multi[row][col] = 1;
*(*(multi + row) + col) = 1;
It is also true that the following two notations give the same result:

multi[row]  *(multi + row) Since the one on the right must evaluate to a pointer, the array notation on the left must also do so. In fact multi[0] will return a pointer to the first integer in the first row, multi[1] a pointer to the first integer of the second row, etc. Actually multi[n] evaluates to a pointer to that array of integers that constitutes the nth row of our two-dimensional array. This means we can think of multi as an array of arrays, and multi[n] as a pointer to the nth array of this array of arrays.
PROGRAM 9.1
/* Program 9.1 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#define COLS 5
typedef int Row_array[COLS];
Row_array *rptr;
for (row = 0; row < nrows; row++) for (col = 0; col < COLS; col++)
rptr[row][col] = 17;
return 0;

Using this method, rptr has all the characteristics of an array name (except that rptr is modifiable), and array notation can be used throughout the rest of the program. This also means that if you intend to write a function to modify the array contents, COLS must be used as part of the parameter of that function, just as was done when discussing passing two-dimensional arrays to a function.
METHOD 2
In Method 1 above, rptr became a pointer of type "one-dimensional array of COLS integers". It is evident then that there is a syntax to use this type without needing the typedef keyword. If we write:
int (*xptr)[COLS];

the variable xptr would have the same characteristics as variable rptr from Method 1, and we will not have used the typedef keyword. Here xptr is a pointer to an array of integers, and the size of that array is given by the constant COLS. The parentheses cause the pointer notation to take precedence, even though array notation has higher evaluation precedence. That is, had we written:

int *xptr[COLS]; we would have defined xptr as an array of pointers consisting of a number of pointers equal to the amount defined by COLS. Obviously this is not the same thing. However, arrays of pointers are useful when dynamically allocating two-dimensional arrays, as we will see in the following two methods.
METHOD 3

Consider the case where we do not know the number of elements per row at compile time — that is, both the number of rows and the number of columns will be determined at runtime. One way to do this is to create an array of integer pointers and then reserve memory for each row, pointing these pointers at each row. Consider:
PROGRAM 9.2
/* Program 9.2 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
int **rowptr;
rowptr = malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
if (rowptr == NULL)
for (row = 0; row < nrows; row++)

{
rowptr[row] = malloc(ncols * sizeof(int));
if (rowptr[row] == NULL)
if (row > 0)

printf(" %d", ((int)rowptr[row] - (int)rowptr[row-1]));
}
return 0;
In the code above, rowptr is a pointer to a pointer of integer type. In this case it points to the first element of an array of int pointers. Consider the number of calls to malloc():

To get our array of pointers: 1 call To get space for the rows: 5 calls Total: 6 calls If you opt for this method, note that while you can use array notation to access individual elements of the array (e.g., rowptr[row][col] = 17;), this does not mean that the data in the two-dimensional array is contiguous in memory.

You can however use array notation just as if the data were in a contiguous block of memory. For example, you can write: rowptr[row][col] = 176; just as you would if rowptr were the name of a two-dimensional array created at compile time. Of course, the values of [row] and [col] must be within the established bounds of the array that has been created, the same as with an array created at compile time.
If what we want is a contiguous block of memory dedicated to storing the array elements, this can be done as follows:
METHOD 4

With this method we first reserve a block of memory to hold the complete array. Then we create an array of pointers to point to each row. Thus, although we are using an array of pointers, the actual array in memory is contiguous. The code is:
PROGRAM 9.3
/* Program 9.3 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
int **rptr; int *aptr; int *testptr;
/* reserve memory for the complete array */
aptr = malloc(nrows * ncols * sizeof(int));
if (aptr == NULL)
/* reserve space for row pointers */
rptr = malloc(nrows * sizeof(int *));
if (rptr == NULL)
/* make the pointers point */ for (k = 0; k < nrows; k++)
for (row = 0; row < nrows; row++)

{
printf("\n%d %d", row, rptr[row]);
if (row > 0)
for (row = 0; row < nrows; row++)
for (col = 0; col < ncols; col++)
for (row = 0; row < nrows; row++)
for (col = 0; col < ncols; col++)
return 0;
Consider again the number of calls to malloc(): to reserve memory to hold the complete array: 1 call, to reserve memory for the array of pointers: 1 call, total: 2 calls.

Well, each call to malloc() creates an additional overhead of space since malloc() is generally implemented by the operating system forming a linked list containing the data corresponding to the block size. But more importantly, with large arrays (several hundred rows), keeping track of the memory that must be freed at some point can become tedious. This last method, combined with the convenience of the contiguous memory block — which allows initialization of the entire block to zeros using memset() — would make this alternative the most convenient.

As a final example on the topic of multidimensional arrays, we will demonstrate the dynamic allocation of a 3D array. This example will show one more thing to keep in mind with this type of allocation. For the reasons stated above, we will use the last method. See the following code:
PROGRAM 9.4
/* Program 9.4 from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
char *space; char ***Arr3D;
int y, z, diff;
/* First, reserve space for the complete array */
space = malloc(X_DIM * Y_DIM * Z_DIM * sizeof(char));
/* next, reserve space for an array of pointers which will eventually each point to the first element of a 2D array of char pointers */
Arr3D = malloc(Z_DIM * sizeof(char **));
/* for each of these assign a pointer to a newly allocated row pointer array */ for (z = 0; z < Z_DIM; z++)

{
Arr3D[z] = malloc(Y_DIM * sizeof(char *));
for (y = 0; y < Y_DIM; y++)

Arr3D[z][y] = space + (z*(X_DIM * Y_DIM) + y*X_DIM);
}
/* check addresses in our 3D array */ for (z = 0; z < Z_DIM; z++)

{
printf("Address of array %d is %ph\n", z, *Arr3D[z]);
for (y = 0; y < Y_DIM; y++)
return 0;

If you have followed this tutorial to this point you should have no problem deciphering the code of the program above based on the comments. There are a couple of things to explain anyway; let us begin with the line that says:
Arr3D[z][y] = space + (z*(X_DIM * Y_DIM) + y*X_DIM);

Note that here space is a character pointer, which is the same type as Arr3D[z][y]. It is important that when adding an integer — such as the one obtained by evaluating the expression (z*(X_DIM * Y_DIM) + y * X_DIM) — to a pointer, the result is a new pointer value. And when we assign pointer values to pointer variables, the data types of the value and the variable must match.
CHAPTER 10: POINTERS TO FUNCTIONS

Up to this point we have discussed the use of pointers with data objects. C also allows the declaration of pointers to functions. Pointers to functions have a variety of uses, and some of these will be covered here.

Consider the following real problem: we have to write a function that is capable of sorting virtually any collection of data that can be contained in an array — whether it is an array of strings, integers, floats, or even structures.

The sorting algorithm can be the same for all. For example, it could be a simple bubble sort algorithm, or the much more complex quick sort or shell sort algorithm. We will use a simple bubble algorithm for our didactic purposes.

Sedgewick [1] has described the bubble sort algorithm using C code by establishing a function that, once given a pointer to the array, sorts the array. If we call this function bubble(), a sort program is described by bubble_1.c in the code that follows:
bubble_1.c
/* Program bubble_1.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
printf("%d ", arr[i]);
return 0;

The bubble sort is one of the simplest sorting algorithms. The algorithm scans the array from the second to the last element, comparing each one with the one preceding it. If the preceding element is greater than the current element, the elements are swapped so that the larger one ends up closer to the end of the array. On the first pass this results in the largest element ending up at the end of the array. The array is then limited to all elements except the last, and the process is repeated. That puts the next largest element in the place preceding the largest. The process repeats a number of times equal to the total number of elements minus 1. The final result is a sorted array.

Here our function is designed to sort an integer array. So we compare integers and use an integer temporary store. What we want to find out is whether we can modify this code so it can be used with any data type — i.e., not restricted to sorting integers.

At the same time we do not want to modify our algorithm or the code associated with it every time we use it. Let us begin by removing the comparison from inside bubble() so that it will be relatively easy to modify the comparison function without having to rewrite pieces related to the sort algorithm itself. This gives us bubble_2.c:
bubble_2.c
/* Program bubble_2.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
for (i = N-1; i >= 0; i--) for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) if (compare(a[j-1], a[j]))

If our goal is to make our sort routine independent of data type, one way to achieve it is to use void (typeless) pointers to point to the data instead of using the integer data type. To address this objective, we will start by modifying some things in our program above, so we can use integer pointers. Let us start by putting in integer type pointers:
bubble_3.c
/* Program bubble_3.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
for (i = N-1; i >= 0; i--) for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) if (compare(&p[j-1], &p[j]))

Notice the changes. We are now passing a pointer to an integer (or to an array of integers) to bubble(). And from within bubble() we are passing pointers to the elements we want to compare from the array to our compare() function. And of course we are dereferencing these pointers in our compare() function so that the actual comparison between elements is made. Our next step will be to convert the pointers in bubble() to void pointers so that the function becomes more type-insensitive. This is shown in bubble_4.c:
bubble_4.c
/* Program bubble_4.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
for (i = N-1; i >= 0; i--) for (j = 1; j <= i; j++) if (compare((void *)&p[j-1], (void *)&p[j]))
int *m1, *n1;

Note that, in doing this, in compare() we had to introduce the cast of the passed void pointer to the actual type being sorted. But, as we will see later, this is fine. And since what is being passed to bubble() is still a pointer to an integer array, we had to convert those pointers to void pointers when passing them as parameters in our call to compare().

Let us now address the problem of what to pass to bubble(). We want to make the first parameter of this function a void pointer. But that means we must do something inside bubble() about the variable t, which is currently of integer type. Also, where we use t = p[j-1];, the type of p[j-1] needs to be known in order to know how many bytes will be copied to the variable t.

Up to now, in bubble_4.c, knowledge inside bubble() of the data type to be sorted (and therefore the individual size of each element) was obtained from the fact that the first parameter was an integer pointer. If we want to be able to use bubble() to sort any type of data, we need to make this parameter a void pointer. But by doing so, we lose the information about the individual size of the elements within the array. So in bubble_5.c we will add an extra parameter to handle the size information.
bubble_5.c
/* Program bubble_5.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */
printf("%ld ", arr[i]);
return 0;
unsigned char buf[4];
unsigned char *bp = p;
if (compare((void *)(bp + width*(j-1)), (void *)(bp + j*width)))
long *m1, *n1;

Note that the array data type has been changed from int to long to show the necessary changes in the compare() function. Inside bubble() we have built a buffer with the variable t (which we had to change from int type to long). We added an unsigned char buffer of size 4, which is the size required to store a long integer. The *bp pointer of unsigned char type is used to point to the start of the array to be sorted.

We also had to modify what is passed to compare(), and how we did the swapping of elements when the comparison indicated a swap should be made. The use of memcpy() and pointer notation goes toward reducing type sensitivity.
Again, carefully reviewing bubble_5.c against bubble_4.c can be very helpful for understanding what is happening and why.

Let us now move to bubble_6.c, where we use the same bubble() function from bubble_5.c, now to sort strings instead of integers. Of course we have had to modify the comparison function since the way to compare strings is different from the way integers are compared. Also in bubble_6.c we have removed the lines that were inside bubble() as comments in bubble_5.c:
bubble_6.c
/* Program bubble_6.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/13/97 */

#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#define MAX_BUF 256
char arr2[5][20] = { "Mickey Mouse",
"Donald Duck", "Minnie Mouse", "Goofy", "Ted Jensen" };
printf("%s\n", arr2[i]);
return 0;
unsigned char buf[MAX_BUF];
unsigned char *bp = p;
k = compare((void *)(bp + width*(j-1)),
(void *)(bp + j*width));
if (k > 0)

The fact that bubble() was not modified from the one used in bubble_5.c indicates that this function is capable of sorting a wide variety of data types. What then needs to be done is to pass to bubble() the name of the comparison function to use, so that it becomes truly universal. Just as the name of an array is the address of its first element in the data segment, the name of a function is interpreted as the address of that function in the code segment. So we need a pointer to a function — in this case, the comparison function.

Pointers to functions must match the functions they point to in the number and types of parameters and in the type of the value returned by the function. In our case, we declare our function pointer as:
int (*fptr)(const void *p1, const void *p2);
Note that if we had written:
int *fptr(const void *p1, const void *p2);

we would get the prototype of a function that returns a pointer to an integer. Because in C parentheses have a higher precedence order than the dereference operator '*', by putting the string (*fptr) in parentheses we indicate that we are declaring a pointer to a function.
Now let us modify our declaration of bubble() by adding a 4th parameter — a pointer to a function of the appropriate type. Its prototype then becomes:
void bubble(void *p, int width, int N, int(*fptr)(const void *, const void *));
When calling bubble(), we will insert the name of the comparison function to use. bubble_7.c demonstrates how this method allows the same bubble() function to be used to sort data of different types:
bubble_7.c
/* Program bubble_7.c from PTRTUT10.HTM 6/10/97 */

#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#define MAX_BUF 256

long arr[10] = { 3,6,1,2,3,8,4,1,7,2 };
char arr2[5][20] = { "Mickey Mouse", "Donald Duck",
"Minnie Mouse", "Goofy", "Ted Jensen" };

printf("%ld ", arr[i]);
puts("\n");

printf("%d ", arr[i]);
puts("\n");
printf("%s\n", arr2[i]);
return 0;

}
void bubble(void *p, int width, int N,
int(*fptr)(const void *, const void *))

{
int i, j, k;
unsigned char buf[MAX_BUF];
unsigned char *bp = p;
k = fptr((void *)(bp + width*(j-1)),
(void *)(bp + j*width));
if (k > 0)
long *m1, *n1;
References for Chapter 10:
[1] Robert Sedgewick, "Algorithms in C", Addison-Wesley, ISBN 0-201-51425-7
EPILOGUE
I wrote this material to provide an introduction to pointers for those beginning in C. In C, the more one understands about pointers, the greater the flexibility gained for writing code.

The content of this work expands what was my first effort, named ptr_help.txt, which was found in one of the first versions of Bob Stout's C SNIPPETS collection. The content of this version has been updated from that found as PTRTUTOT.ZIP included in the file SNIP9510.ZIP.

I am always happy to receive constructive criticism of this material, or revisions or requests for the addition of other important material.
